claim: Enterprises care about outcomes/workflows, not which LLM powers them; abstract the model away and swap models as better ones emerge without changing the product
actionable: When selling B2B AI products, build around the business outcome (e.g., 'automate contract review') not the model choice; users won't care if you swap Claude for GPT-4 behind the scenes

claim: Avoid building products that could be incorporated directly into foundational models; focus on software that requires significant proprietary business logic and customer data integration
actionable: Before building an AI wrapper, ask: 'Could ChatGPT or Claude subsume this directly?' If yes, add significant proprietary workflows, integrations, or domain expertise to create defensibility

claim: Enterprise buyers distinguish between 'context' (necessary operational functions like HR/ERP that any vendor can provide) and 'core' (unique IP that differentiates the business)
actionable: Build proprietary AI in your 'core' business value (e.g., drug discovery for biotech, recommendation engines for Netflix); outsource 'context' functions (HR systems) to proven software vendors

claim: As model costs converge to zero, software margins expand (Box: 81% gross margin); competitors differentiate on the abstraction layer around the model, not the model itself
actionable: Plan for token costs to become commoditized; your moat is the integration layer, compliance/governance features, domain-specific logic, and seamless UX—not the LLM

claim: Enterprises are at ~10% adoption of general chat assistants and ~1% adoption of autonomous agents; majority of value will come from ISV solutions, not internal builds
actionable: If building B2B AI, expect that most enterprises will buy pre-built ISV solutions for common workflows rather than building from scratch; focus on out-of-the-box domain solutions

claim: Usage-based pricing for AI workflows is often better than annual contracts because underlying compute is elastic and unpredictable, unlike traditional software
actionable: For AI automation replacing services/BPO, consider usage-based billing (per workflow run, per output) instead of annual contracts; margins still grow as costs decline

claim: The TAM for software will expand 5-10x because AI enables *new workflows* that enterprises didn't automate before, not just digitalizing existing labor (Jevons Paradox dynamics)
actionable: When forecasting, don't assume zero-sum displacement of labor; estimate new use cases AI enables (e.g., contract review now viable for 80% of deals instead of 5%) to project TAM growth

claim: Reasoning models improve agent orchestration (chaining multiple agents), but testing shows some edge cases where they perform worse than non-reasoning models for unclear reasons
actionable: When building multi-agent systems, test reasoning models, but benchmark against non-reasoning baselines on your specific workflows to avoid over-reliance on newer models

claim: Enterprise AI adoption parallels cloud adoption (early skeptics, regulatory/security concerns), but with a critical difference: AI is visible to end-users and drives competitive pressure, not just efficiency
actionable: When pitching to enterprises, emphasize competitive risk (competitors already using AI will outpace you) more than cost savings; this drives faster adoption than infrastructure arguments

claim: Model companies without significant software/consumer/enterprise distribution (e.g., pure-play model licensing) face commoditization pressure from open-source alternatives (Meta, DeepSeek)
actionable: Don't start a pure-play LLM company expecting only licensing revenue; pair it with software (APIs), consumer products, or enterprise solutions to create defensibility

claim: CTOs/AI teams at enterprises care about model differences and prefer specific models (e.g., Claude for orchestration); line-of-business execs and end-users don't care which model is used
actionable: When selling enterprise AI, focus sales on CTOs/AI teams for technical credibility, but design products so line-of-business users don't see or care about model selection

claim: A single successful AI startup (example: $12M ARR) switched models multiple times without losing customers; customers cared only that accuracy/SLA targets were met and costs decreased
actionable: When selecting models for production, prioritize SLA/accuracy guarantees and cost trends over moat from a specific model; be prepared to swap models as better/cheaper alternatives emerge

claim: As model costs drop, software margins on top expand 3-4x (e.g., 30% → 60% → 80%); the moat shifts entirely from the model to the software stack and integration layer
actionable: Price AI products so margins expand as token costs fall; reinvest savings into product improvements (not margin hoarding) to stay competitive

claim: Enterprises building homegrown AI focus on 'core' IP (drug discovery, personalization engines); 'context' functions (CRM, HR, ERP) will be bought from ISVs, not built internally
actionable: As an ISV, focus on 'context' functions (operations, compliance, integration) for horizontal sales; help enterprises focus internal AI resources on core differentiation

claim: Chat-based interfaces for internal AI tools are currently popular but may be temporary; traditional UI/dashboards are still preferable for many workflows to reduce prompt engineering overhead
actionable: Don't assume chat is always better; validate that chat UX reduces user friction vs. dashboards for your specific use case; avoid reinventing wheels via prompting
